subject: mathematics
prose_summary:
Section 10.1 recalls integer addition and subtraction and applies signed numbers to model movement and direction. It opens with Rakesh's puzzle — find two numbers given their sum (25) and difference (11) — solved by systematic guess-and-check tabulation of first number, second number, sum, and difference rather than by any formula, then extended to a negative difference (-11), where the section observes that simply swapping the pair from the first puzzle (7 and 18) gives the answer. A Figure it Out set asks for several more sum-and-difference pairs, including cases with negative sums and negative differences, and the puzzle is turned into a partner game that students can later perform as a 'magic trick'. The section then builds the carrom-coin model: a coin struck rightward by a units and then by b units lands at P = a + b, and once strikes can go in either direction, rightward is taken as positive and leftward as negative, so that a rightward strike of 5 followed by a leftward strike of 7 gives 5 + (-7) = -2, placing the coin two units to the left of zero. This models two pieces of information at once — magnitude (distance) and direction (sign) — on the number line, and students answer questions about a missing movement given the final position and about the final position after a chain of strikes 1, -2, 3, -4, …, -10, and compare magnitudes and directions from given diagrams. The section then recalls the Grade 6 token model — a green token for +1, a red token for -1, the two together forming a zero pair that cancels — and uses it to compute (+7) - (+18): since there are not enough positives to remove eighteen, eleven zero pairs are added first, then 18 positives are removed, leaving 11 negatives, or -11. This reinforces that subtracting a number is the same as adding its additive inverse, with the additive inverse of an integer a written as -a, so that 7 - 18 = 7 + (-18) and 4 - (-12) = 4 + 12.

Section 10.2 reasons out the rules and properties of integer multiplication and division and applies them to expressions and real contexts. It extends the token model from addition to multiplication: 4 × 2 means placing two green tokens into an empty bag four times (giving 8), while 4 × (-2) places two red tokens four times (giving -8). When the multiplier is negative, tokens are removed instead of placed, so (-4) × 2 requires putting in four sets of two zero pairs and then removing the positives, leaving -8; and (-4) × (-2) removes negatives to leave +8. These four cases — 4×2=8, 4×(-2)=-8, (-4)×2=-8, (-4)×(-2)=8 — are then confirmed by a sequence of multiplication patterns, where for a positive multiplicand each unit decrease in the multiplier decreases the product by the multiplicand, and for a negative multiplicand it increases the product, with the pattern extended smoothly below zero and displayed in the four 'times 3' tables. From these the sign rules are stated: like signs give a positive product, unlike signs a negative product, and the magnitude is unaffected by sign. The section establishes 1 × a = a and -1 × a = -a for all integers, then argues that multiplication is commutative (a × b = b × a) by separating magnitude and sign and checking that neither changes on swapping. It introduces Brahmagupta's Brāhmasphuṭasiddhānta (628 CE) rules, which use fortune (dhana) for positive and debt (ṛṇa) for negative quantities and give the first articulation of sign rules for both multiplication and division. Two worked examples apply this — exam marking with positive and negative marks (Example 1) and a mining-shaft elevator moving above and below ground, modelled both by subtraction and by signed speed (Example 2). The section presents a magic-grid game whose product is invariant to the numbers chosen, then converts division into a multiplication question ('what times 25 gives -100?') to derive the division sign rules a ÷ -b = -(a÷b), -a ÷ b = -(a÷b), and -a ÷ -b = a÷b. It then studies expressions: it shows integer multiplication is associative and distributive over addition using regrouping and rectangular token arrangements of green and red tokens, verifies associativity by evaluating a product such as 25 × -6 × 12 in several groupings and orders, works out the sign of a product of many integers from repeated multiplication by -1, and explores 'pattern machines' that compute a + b - c and invite students to build their own. Its extensive Figure it Out sets apply all of this to profit-and-loss, freezing temperatures, a modified integer Collatz iteration, an alien 'pibs' currency puzzle with only +13 and -9 coins, the ordering of large signed expressions, and open-ended maximum/minimum and fill-in-the-blank tasks, and the chapter closes with the traditional board game Terhüchü from Assam and Nagaland.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: An exam has 50 multiple-choice questions with +5 for each correct and -2 for each wrong answer. Mala has 30 correct and 20 wrong: 30 × 5 + 20 × (-2) = 150 + (-40) = 110 marks.

A mining-shaft elevator uses positive integers above ground and negative below. (a) Descending 3 m/min from 0 for one hour: 60 × (-3) = -180 (180 m below). (b) Descending from 15 m for 45 minutes: 15 + 45 × (-3) = -120 (120 m below).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 10.1 p.25
enumerated_exercises.description: Find pairs of numbers from their sums and differences: (a) Sum 27, Diff 9; (b) Sum 4, Diff 12; (c) Sum 0, Diff 10; (d) Sum 0, Diff -10; (e) Sum -7, Diff -1; (f) Sum -7, Diff -13. Then play the sum-and-difference guessing game with a partner.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: section 10.1 p.26–27
enumerated_exercises.description: Using the carrom-coin model P = a + b: (1) if the first movement is -4 and the final position is 5, find the second movement; (2) for successive movements 1, -2, 3, -4, …, -10, find the final position of the coin.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.2 p.31
enumerated_exercises.description: Using the token interpretation, find the values of (a) 3 × (-2); (b) (-5) × (-2); (c) (-4) × (-1); (d) (-7) × 3.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 10.2 p.31
Figure it Out Q3, section 10.2 p.31
enumerated_exercises.description: Try to frame a simple rule to multiply two integers, and confirm that different token sets all representing -2 give the same product when placed into the bag 4 times; check the same for 5 × 4 with different token sets for 4.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out, section 10.2 p.33–34
Figure it Out Q1, section 10.2 p.38
Figure it Out Q2, section 10.2 p.38
enumerated_exercises.description: A freezing process lowers room temperature from 32°C at 5°C per hour. Find the room temperature 10 hours after the process begins.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 10.2 p.38
enumerated_exercises.description: A cement company earns ₹8 profit per white-cement bag and ₹5 loss per grey-cement bag. (a) Find profit or loss on 3,000 white and 5,000 grey bags. (b) If 6,400 grey bags are sold, find how many white bags give neither profit nor loss.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.2 p.38
enumerated_exercises.description: Replace the blank with an integer to make a true statement: (a) (-3) × __ = 27; (b) 5 × __ = -35; (c) __ × (-8) = -56; (d) __ × (-12) = 132; (e) __ ÷ (-8) = 7; (f) __ ÷ 12 = -11.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 10.2 p.42
Figure it Out Q2, section 10.2 p.42
Figure it Out Q3, section 10.2 p.42
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the integer whose product with (-1) is (a) 27; (b) -31; (c) -1; (d) 1; (e) 0.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 10.2 p.42
enumerated_exercises.description: Given 47 - 56 + 14 - 8 + 2 - 8 + 5 = -4, find the value of -47 + 56 - 14 + 8 - 2 + 8 - 5 without calculating the full expression.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 10.2 p.42
enumerated_exercises.description: Try a modified integer Collatz process: start with any number; if even, halve it; if odd, multiply by -3 and add 1; repeat. Try starting numbers -21, -6, etc., and describe the patterns observed.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 10.2 p.43
enumerated_exercises.description: In a test with +4 for correct and -2 for incorrect answers: (a) Anita answered all questions and scored 40 with 15 correct — find her incorrect answers and the number of questions. (b) Anil scored -10 with 5 correct — find his incorrect answers and whether he left any unanswered.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q7, section 10.2 p.43
enumerated_exercises.description: Pick the pattern: find the operations done by the machine shown.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q8, section 10.2 p.43
enumerated_exercises.description: The temperature drops 5°C each hour and is currently 8°C. Write an expression for the temperature after 4 hours.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q9, section 10.2 p.43
enumerated_exercises.description: Find 3 consecutive numbers with a product of (a) -6; (b) 120.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q10, section 10.2 p.43–44
enumerated_exercises.description: An alien currency 'pibs' has only +13 and -9 coins. Show +85 is possible (ten +13 and five -9), then make (a) +20; (b) +40; (c) -50; (d) +8; (e) +10; (f) -2; (g) +1; and (h) decide whether 1568 pibs is possible.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q11, section 10.2 p.44
Figure it Out Q12, section 10.2 p.44
Figure it Out Q13, section 10.2 p.44
Figure it Out Q14, section 10.2 p.44
enumerated_exercises.description: Given 207 × (-33 + 7) = -5382, write the value of -207 × (33 - 7).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q15, section 10.2 p.44
enumerated_exercises.description: Use the numbers 3, -2, 5, -6 exactly once and the operations +, -, × exactly once (with brackets as needed) to write an expression giving (a) the maximum possible result; (b) the minimum possible result.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q16, section 10.2 p.44
enumerated_exercises.description: Fill in the blanks in at least 5 different ways with integers: (a) __ + __ × __ = -36; (b) (__ - __) × __ = 12; (c) (__ - (__ - __)) = -1.
